title: How Python's GIL Works
description: An interactive visual guide explaining Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) — what it is, why it exists, and how it affects threading and multiprocessing.
codeBlock:
import threading

def task(n):
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        total += i
    return total

# Two threads, but only one runs at a time
# due to the GIL
details:
The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is a synchronization mutex used by the reference CPython implementation to ensure that only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time. This lock is central to Python's memory management, protecting CPython internals (specifically reference counting tables) from race conditions. While it simplifies implementation and C extension integration, it restricts CPU-bound multi-threaded programs to single-core execution.

CPython Concurrency & Evolution

  • Reference Counting Memory Safety: Without the GIL, reference counters for every object would require fine-grained lock protections, causing massive overhead for single-threaded tasks. The GIL acts as a coarse-grained lock to guarantee garbage collection thread safety efficiently.
  • I/O Cooperativeness: The GIL does not lock up threads doing network requests or file operations. Blocking system calls voluntarily release the GIL, letting other threads run concurrently.
  • PEP 684 (Per-Interpreter GIL): Standardized in Python 3.12, this allows developers to run multiple independent subinterpreters within a single OS process, each with its own GIL, enabling true parallel multicore Python execution.
  • PEP 703 (Making the GIL Optional): Standardized in Python 3.13, this specifies a compiler-flag configuration (free-threaded mode) to build CPython without the GIL, relying on biased reference counting and thread-safe allocators (mimalloc) instead.

CPython Internals & Reference Specs

  • PEP 703 — Making the GIL Optional in CPython — The specification for removing the GIL and implementing mimalloc with biased reference counting.
  • PEP 684 — A Per-Interpreter GIL — Design for subinterpreters running concurrently on separate OS threads.
  • David Beazley's GIL Visualization — The classic study analyzing GIL latency and multicore thread battle issues.

To visually explore how the GIL serializes execution on multi-core architectures vs how I/O releases the lock, see the Interactive GIL Guide.
